subject: science
topicLabel: Interaction of forces (Frictional force, gravitational force, elastic spring force)
title: Forces in PSLE Science

stats.headline: **Forces are the #2 most-tested PSLE Science topic across 2016-2025** — 58 questions in our archive, with 6 four-mark OEQs and one 5-mark killer. The trap isn't the concept — it's that students forget to NAME all the forces acting at the same time, or skip the comparison phrase.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Forces in PSLE Science
keyConcepts.body: Forces show up in almost every Booklet B paper. The questions look easy in P5 ("which way does the ball move?"), but P6 PSLE raises the bar — you need to NAME every force, COMPARE their sizes, and trace the ENERGY chain.
bullets: **Gravity** — pulls everything down towards the Earth. Always present whenever an object has weight.
**Friction** — opposes motion between two surfaces in contact. **Still acts on a stationary object** — most students miss this.
**Elastic / Spring force** — stored when a spring is compressed or stretched. Released as kinetic energy.
**Magnetic force** — between magnets and magnetic materials. Can attract OR repel; can act without contact.

**The 4 patterns this class drills:**
• Pattern A — Naming ALL forces (incl. friction on stationary)
• Pattern B — Comparing two forces (greater than / equal / less)
• Pattern C — Spring energy chain + stiffness comparison
• Pattern D — Magnets attraction, repulsion, and the balance trick

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Name ALL the forces acting
keyConcepts.body: When a question shows a single object — at rest OR moving — you must list EVERY force on it. The most-missed force: friction on a stationary object. If two surfaces are in contact, friction is there even when nothing is moving.
bullets: **Gravity** — anything with weight has gravity pulling it down.
**Friction** — anything resting on or sliding across a surface has friction (parallel to the surface, opposing motion or potential motion).
**Elastic** — anything attached to a spring/rubber band.
**Magnetic** — anything near a magnet AND made of magnetic material.
**Worked example (real PSLE 2021 Q20):** Zhiming placed a magnet and a wooden block on a slope. The wooden block did not move. Which force(s) acted on the wooden block?
✅ Correct: **gravity** (down the slope) + **friction** (parallel to slope, holding it still) + **magnetic** (from the nearby magnet).
❌ Common wrong: gravity + magnetic only — students drop friction because the block isn't moving. WRONG. Friction is exactly what's holding it still against gravity.
**Mantra**: stationary ≠ no forces. Stationary = forces are **balanced**. Always at LEAST gravity + friction.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Comparing two forces (greater / equal / less)
keyConcepts.body: When an object MOVES or STOPS, the marker wants you to explain WHY in terms of comparing two forces. The verbal template is fixed: "The [force X] is greater than the [force Y], so the object [outcome]." Skipping the comparison drops 1 mark.
bullets: **Balanced (equal forces)** → object stays still OR moves at constant speed.
**One force greater than the other** → object accelerates in the direction of the greater force.
**If gravity > friction** on a slope → object slides down.
**If friction > push** → object doesn't move when pushed.
**If elastic spring force > weight** → object launched up.
**Worked example (real PSLE 2018 Q37, 3 marks):** Seng Huat's phone slipped out of its charger plug after some time. Why?
✅ Full marks: "The **weight** of the phone is **greater than** the **friction** between the plug and the phone, so the phone slips out."
❌ Common wrong: "Gravity pulls the phone down." — right idea, but missing the comparison phrase loses the marking point.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Spring energy chain (PE → KE → GPE)
keyConcepts.body: Spring problems ALWAYS chain energies. Compressed/stretched spring stores **elastic PE**. Released → object gains **kinetic energy**. Object rises → kinetic converts to **gravitational PE**. Name ALL three steps explicitly.
bullets: **Compressed/stretched spring** = stores ELASTIC potential energy.
**Spring releases** → object speeds up → ELASTIC PE → **KINETIC energy**.
**Object rises** → slows down → KINETIC → **GRAVITATIONAL PE**.
**Stiffer spring** = more elastic PE for the same compression. "Spring Q extends less than spring P for the same mass → spring Q stores more elastic PE."
**Fair-test signal:** when the question says "no wind in the room" or "same starting position", that's a hint to mention WHY — keeps other variables constant.
**Worked example (real PSLE 2021 Q40, 4 marks):** Samy compressed a spring to launch a toy rocket. No wind in the room.
**Part (a) — Relationship between compression and distance:** ✅ "The greater the compression of the spring, the greater the distance moved by the rocket."
**Part (b) — Forces acting on the rocket in mid-flight:** ✅ "Gravitational force and frictional force" (air resistance counts as friction).
**Part (c) — Energy changes during the rocket's flight:** ✅ "Kinetic energy and gravitational potential energy" (KE converts to GPE as rocket rises).
**Part (d) — Why use a sandpit as the landing area:** ✅ "The rocket lands in the sandpit and makes a depression, which serves as a clear marking for the distance to be measured."

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Magnets (attract, repel, balance)
keyConcepts.body: Magnetic force is the only "contactless" force at PSLE. Two big traps: (a) repulsion balancing weight (like-poles facing each other), and (b) when a magnet ATTRACTS something, the force PULLS, when it REPELS, it PUSHES — direction matters.
bullets: **Like poles repel** (N-N or S-S). **Unlike poles attract** (N-S).
**Magnetic materials:** iron, steel, nickel, cobalt. **NOT** aluminium, copper, plastic, wood.
**Strength varies with distance** — closer = stronger force.
**Worked example (real PSLE 2019 Q38, 5 marks):** Leela placed a magnet on a balance. She brought object A close to the magnet (above it). The balance reading INCREASED.
**Part (a) — What is object A?** ✅ "A magnet."

**Part (b) — Why does the balance reading increase?** ✅ "In Diagram 2, due to the **repulsion** between like poles of the two magnets, the magnet exerts a **greater push** on the top of the balance, so the reading increases." (vs Diagram 1 where only the magnet's weight pushes down.)

**Part (c)(i) — If object A is replaced with an iron bar, what happens to the reading?** ✅ "The reading **decreases** (option P). The iron bar **attracts** the magnet upwards, so the magnet pushes down on the balance with a smaller force."
**Part (c)(ii) — Variable to investigate to make the change bigger:** ✅ "The **distance** between the magnet and object A (or the iron bar). The closer the distance, the stronger the magnetic force."
**The key insight**: for balance / floating-magnet questions, ALWAYS state both forces, state which is greater (or that they're equal), THEN conclude. "Repel" alone is not enough.

**💡 Bonus mark — when the question asks "how to make results more reliable?":** answer with either (1) **repeat the experiment and take an average**, OR (2) **keep other variables constant** (e.g. distance, position, temperature). Almost always worth 1 bonus mark in experiment-based questions.

keyConcepts.title: Let's get some practice!
keyConcepts.body: Time to put it together. This quiz pulls real PSLE-style force questions across all 4 patterns.
bullets: **10 MCQ + 5 OEQ** — a mix across friction, gravity, elastic, and magnets.
After you submit, the AI looks at where you went wrong and suggests a follow-up focused quiz on just those patterns.
Mistakes auto-saved — we'll come back to them in 1 week.

cta.label: Click here to start your practice quiz

keyWords.definition: The force that pulls everything down towards the Earth. Always acts on objects with weight, regardless of whether they're moving or stationary.
The force that opposes motion (or attempted motion) between two surfaces in contact. ALWAYS present when surfaces touch — even when the object is stationary, friction is the force holding it still.
Energy stored in a compressed or stretched spring/rubber band. Released as kinetic energy when the spring returns to its natural shape.
Energy a moving object has. Increases with speed and mass. Often comes FROM elastic PE (spring release) and converts INTO gravitational PE (rising) or heat (friction).
Energy an object has because of its height above the ground. Increases as the object rises, decreases as it falls.
When two or more forces acting on an object cancel out (equal sizes, opposite directions). Result: object stays still OR moves at constant speed.
The force between two like poles (N-N or S-S) that pushes them apart. Can be used to balance gravity in 'floating magnet' setups.

subTopics.label: Pattern A — Naming all forces (incl. stationary friction)
subTopics.description: Tests whether the student names EVERY force acting on an object — gravity always, friction whenever surfaces touch (even at rest), elastic if attached to spring, magnetic if near a magnet. Common at-rest trap: students drop friction when the object isn't moving.

subTopics.label: Pattern B — Comparing forces (greater than / equal / less)
subTopics.description: Tests stating which of two forces is greater and explaining the outcome (object moves / stops / stays still). The phrase 'greater than' is a marking-point keyword. Balanced forces → stationary or constant-speed motion.

subTopics.label: Pattern C — Spring energy chain (PE → KE → GPE)
subTopics.description: Tests the elastic PE → KE → GPE conversion when a compressed spring launches an object. Must name all three energy types. Stiffer spring stores more elastic PE per unit compression.

subTopics.label: Pattern D — Magnets (attract, repel, balance)
subTopics.description: Tests like-poles-repel / unlike-attract rule, identifying magnetic materials, and the balance trick where repulsion holds a magnet up against gravity. Strength decreases with distance.
